directions.scope: Characterize what happens if identification, gatekeeping, finance, and placement rules are left unchanged while demographic decline and workforce aging continue. Bounded to describing drift and its distributional consequences; does not propose intervention.
directions.title: Baseline: no new SEN policy, observe passive trajectory

directions.scope: Focus on measurement: reconciling inconsistent statistical series, disentangling prevalence change from diagnostic-practice, access, and incentive effects, and testing county variation. Bounded to evidence and monitoring architecture, not on changing service or placement rules.
directions.title: Verify and reconcile the identification trend and its drivers

directions.scope: Options touching the definitional and diagnostic structure — SNI/BTMN category boundaries, expert-committee gatekeeping, waiting lists, and non-categorical/tiered alternatives. Bounded to how identification is defined and adjudicated, drawing on comparative reforms as transferability-tested evidence only.
directions.title: Redesign category and gatekeeping architecture

directions.scope: Options addressing per-pupil weighting, normative multipliers, and census- versus diagnosis-gated allocation as drivers of identification. Bounded to funding-design mechanisms and their incentive effects, not to service delivery capacity itself.
directions.title: Realign finance incentives away from diagnosis-gated funding

directions.scope: Options addressing gyógypedagógus shortages, regional maldistribution, traveling networks, co-teaching infrastructure, teacher readiness, and the assess-to-serve gap. Bounded to workforce and support-delivery capacity, not to identification rules or finance formulas.
directions.title: Build specialist and mainstream support capacity

directions.scope: Options confronting how SEN identification interacts with free school choice, parental mobility, institutional selectivity, and Roma over-classification to reshape school composition. Bounded to compositional-sorting and anti-segregation mechanisms, including legal-accountability obligations.
directions.title: Address sorting, school choice, and segregation interaction

directions.scope: Options recognizing that non-SEN policies — school-readiness/entry-age rules, rural school-network structure, private/church school growth — measurably move SEN identification and capacity. Bounded to coordinating these adjacent levers, not to SEN-specific rule changes covered elsewhere.
directions.title: Coordinate adjacent-domain policy levers affecting identification

rejected_framings.framing: Adopt Finland's tiered support model as the recommended solution for Hungary
rejected_framings.reason: Out of scope: the brief forbids endorsing international models as solutions and requires explicit transferability tests. Finnish evidence itself shows persistent regional variation, low actual inclusion of Tier 3 students, documentation burden, and cost-driven tier assignment — so presenting it as a ready answer would prejudge outcomes.

rejected_framings.framing: Treat the identification rise as established over-diagnosis requiring caps or tightening
rejected_framings.reason: Prejudges causation the brief requires research to verify; magnitude, continuity, and drivers are contested, and Polish structural rollback shows abrupt reversal produces implementation chaos rather than resolution. Framing as confirmed over-diagnosis would collapse the option space prematurely.

rejected_framings.framing: Frame the issue primarily as a clinical diagnostic-protocol problem
rejected_framings.reason: Clinical diagnostic protocol design is explicitly out of scope; evidence indicates classification functions as a financing/service-eligibility and administrative category rather than a purely clinical determination, so a clinical framing misrepresents the decision-relevant terrain.
